executive_brief:
The threat landscape has crossed a critical threshold. Over the past week, AI-driven vulnerability discovery has compressed the traditional patching lifecycle from months to hours, while a cascading series of unpatched Linux kernel flaws has left enterprise infrastructure exposed to deterministic privilege escalation. Simultaneously, the ShinyHunters extortion group executed a massive breach of the Canvas educational platform, exfiltrating 3.65 terabytes of data and forcing a ransom payment after demonstrating persistent re-entry capabilities. The software supply chain is no longer a secondary concern; TeamPCP’s automated CI/CD poisoning campaign has transformed developer workstations into the primary beachhead, compromising build pipelines, cloud secrets, and AI training environments simultaneously.

What changed this week is the speed and autonomy of the attack surface. Frontier AI models are now autonomously identifying semantic logic flaws and generating functional zero-day exploits, including a confirmed AI-developed 2FA bypass deployed in the wild. Network control planes are under coordinated assault, with actively exploited zero-days in Palo Alto PAN-OS, Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, and an 18-year-old NGINX vulnerability being weaponized in real-time. Security teams must abandon reactive patching SLAs and implement near-real-time validation, strict kernel hardening, and behavioral monitoring for autonomous AI agents. The window for containment has collapsed.

threat_stories.headline: The Linux Kernel Fracture: A Cascading Privilege Escalation Crisis
threat_stories.narrative:
The Linux kernel is experiencing a systemic stability failure. Over three weeks, attackers and researchers have exposed a relentless sequence of local privilege escalation vulnerabilities that bypass traditional memory protection mechanisms. The cascade began with Copy Fail, which abused AF_ALG sockets to write directly to the page cache. This was immediately followed by Dirty Frag, a deterministic exploit chaining xfrm-ESP and RxRPC flaws to achieve root access without race conditions. Within days, Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) emerged as a direct sequel, exploiting ESP-in-TCP processing to corrupt in-memory file data. These are not isolated bugs; they represent a fundamental flaw in how the kernel handles memory fragmentation and cryptographic operations.

Public proof-of-concepts are circulating, and traditional file integrity monitoring fails because the exploits modify data in RAM, not on disk. Attackers can overwrite protected files like /usr/bin/su or /etc/passwd in memory, tricking privileged processes into executing malicious code. The exploit chain is highly reliable, avoids kernel panics, and operates across all major distributions. This creates a persistent, low-barrier path to full host compromise for any attacker with basic local access.

The vulnerability lifecycle has collapsed. Kernel maintainers are recommending temporary kill switches to unload affected modules, but the underlying architectural weaknesses require extensive refactoring. Until comprehensive patches are backported, every Linux host remains a ticking time bomb for lateral movement and ransomware deployment.

timeline.event: Copy Fail vulnerability disclosed, demonstrating page cache write primitive via AF_ALG sockets.
timeline.significance: Established the foundational technique for in-memory file modification without disk writes.

timeline.event: Dirty Frag proof-of-concept published, chaining CVE-2026-43284 and CVE-2026-43500.
timeline.significance: Demonstrated deterministic root escalation across major distributions, bypassing traditional mitigations.

timeline.event: Microsoft and Linux vendors release patches for Dirty Frag components.
timeline.significance: Temporary mitigation deployed, but underlying memory handling flaws remain exposed.

timeline.event: Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) disclosed with functional PoC targeting ESP-in-TCP subsystem.
timeline.significance: Confirmed the patching cycle failed to resolve the root architectural defect, leaving systems vulnerable again.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. All major Linux distributions (Ubuntu, RHEL, Debian, SUSE, Amazon Linux) are affected. Any local user with basic shell access can achieve root privileges, completely compromising host integrity and enabling lateral movement.
threat_stories.action_required: Immediately blocklist esp4, esp6, and rxrpc kernel modules using modprobe.d configurations. Apply vendor kernel patches and reboot all systems. Implement strict seccomp profiles to restrict syscall combinations. Disable unnecessary local shell access on production servers and enforce mandatory access controls (SELinux/AppArmor) to limit process capabilities.

threat_stories.headline: The AI Tipping Point: Autonomous Vulnerability Discovery and Weaponized Zero-Days
threat_stories.narrative:
The threat landscape has fundamentally shifted. Frontier AI models are no longer assisting developers; they are autonomously discovering and weaponizing vulnerabilities at machine speed. Anthropic’s Mythos and Microsoft’s MDASH are proactively scanning codebases, identifying hundreds of flaws in near real-time. Simultaneously, threat actors are leveraging AI to generate functional zero-day exploits. Google Threat Intelligence Group confirmed the first AI-developed zero-day in the wild: a semantic logic flaw that bypasses two-factor authentication in a popular open-source admin tool. The exploit contained AI-specific artifacts like hallucinated CVSS scores and educational strings, proving it was generated by a language model analyzing developer intent.

This marks the end of the traditional patching cycle. Attackers can now parse documentation, identify logical contradictions in authentication flows, and deploy working payloads within hours of disclosure. Palo Alto Networks disclosed 26 CVEs representing 75 distinct issues found via AI scanning, while Mozilla resolved 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox identified by Mythos. The volume and velocity of discoveries mean static posture controls are obsolete. Exploitation is outpacing remediation, and legacy systems are being targeted en masse.

The blast radius extends beyond traditional software. AI agents are being granted excessive permissions, and prompt injection attacks are bypassing guardrails to exfiltrate data and trigger downstream automations. Without behavioral monitoring and runtime validation, autonomous agents will continue to escalate their own privileges and manipulate critical workflows.

timeline.event: GTIG detects first AI-generated zero-day exploit in the wild targeting 2FA bypass.
timeline.significance: Confirms AI can autonomously identify semantic logic flaws and generate functional payloads.

timeline.event: Microsoft MDASH AI system identifies 16 Windows vulnerabilities patched in Patch Tuesday.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates AI-driven vulnerability discovery is now integrated into vendor patch cycles.

timeline.event: Palo Alto Networks discloses 26 CVEs (75 issues) found via Mythos AI scanning.
timeline.significance: Highlights the scale of AI-assisted discovery across enterprise security products.

timeline.event: Mythos identifies 271 vulnerabilities in Mozilla Firefox; AI-generated exploit tools proliferate.
timeline.significance: Confirms AI scanning is uncovering flaws that evaded decades of human review and automated testing.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Enterprise software, identity systems, web browsers, and AI agent infrastructures are under immediate threat. The window between vulnerability disclosure and active exploitation has collapsed to hours.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement behavioral detection frameworks for AI systems and autonomous agents. Enforce strict least-privilege access and runtime monitoring for all machine identities. Transition to near-real-time automated patching and configuration management. Deploy virtual patching and zero-trust network architectures to limit lateral movement. Audit and restrict AI tool permissions immediately.

threat_stories.headline: The Canvas Catastrophe: ShinyHunters Hold Global Education Hostage
threat_stories.narrative:
Educational infrastructure faced a coordinated, devastating assault by the ShinyHunters extortion group. Exploiting a vulnerability in Instructure’s Free-for-Teacher environment, attackers breached the Canvas LMS platform, exfiltrating 3.65 terabytes of data spanning nearly 9,000 institutions. The theft included student IDs, private messages, enrollment records, and faculty contact information. When Instructure initially patched the flaw and refused to pay, ShinyHunters demonstrated persistent access, slipping back through residual misconfigurations to deface login portals and disrupt final exams across multiple countries.

The group escalated to direct extortion, deploying on-screen ransom messages and threatening a massive data dump. The breach forced Instructure to reach a ransom agreement to halt the leak, highlighting the catastrophic risk of under-resourced free-tier environments and the failure of traditional containment strategies against agile extortion gangs. The US government has initiated an investigation, and congressional committees are demanding testimony on the platform's security posture.

This incident exposes a critical architectural flaw: shared infrastructure models where free-tier vulnerabilities provide direct pathways to production environments. Attackers leveraged API export features and compromised authentication tokens to bypass initial containment, proving that patching a single CVE is insufficient without comprehensive identity governance and network segmentation.

timeline.event: Initial breach detected via Free-for-Teacher vulnerability; 3.65TB data exfiltration begins.
timeline.significance: Establishes initial access and massive data theft across nearly 9,000 institutions.

timeline.event: ShinyHunters deface ~330 login portals; nationwide service outage disrupts final exams.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates persistent access and escalation from data theft to direct extortion.

timeline.event: Instructure reaches ransom agreement; stolen data reportedly returned and deleted.
timeline.significance: Forced financial capitulation highlights the operational and reputational cost of the breach.

timeline.event: US House Committee on Homeland Security initiates investigation; FBI issues alert.
timeline.significance: Elevates incident to national infrastructure risk, triggering regulatory scrutiny.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Nearly 9,000 educational institutions and 30 million students/staff affected. Massive PII exposure, operational disruption during critical academic periods, and forced ransom payment.
threat_stories.action_required: Temporarily disable Free-for-Teacher accounts and restrict public-facing Canvas admin endpoints. Rotate all Canvas API tokens, SSO credentials, and internal cryptographic keys. Implement strict WAF rules to block known malicious IP ranges and monitor API export features. Conduct full incident response to eradicate persistence mechanisms and validate patch deployment across all environments.

threat_stories.headline: TeamPCP’s Supply Chain Blitz: Poisoning the Developer Ecosystem
threat_stories.narrative:
The TeamPCP threat group has executed a highly sophisticated, automated assault on the software supply chain, targeting the very tools developers use to build and secure applications. Leveraging a cache-poisoning technique against GitHub Actions, the group compromised the Mini Shai-Hulud worm to hijack CI/CD pipelines. By scraping short-lived OpenID Connect (OIDC) tokens from runner memory, attackers published over 400 malicious packages to npm and PyPI, including trojanized versions of TanStack, Mistral AI SDK, Guardrails AI, and Checkmarx components.

The malware operates as a credential stealer with a destructive dead man’s switch. It harvests cloud tokens, SSH keys, API secrets, and browser data, then monitors a stolen GitHub token. If the token is revoked, the payload triggers a complete local disk wipe, destroying evidence and crippling development environments. This campaign transforms developer workstations into the new primary beachhead, compromising build environments, cloud infrastructure, and AI training pipelines simultaneously.

The attack demonstrates a shift from direct repository takeover to automated pipeline poisoning. By abusing pull_request_target triggers and ephemeral runner configurations, TeamPCP bypassed traditional supply chain security controls. The open-sourcing of the worm on GitHub ensures rapid proliferation, allowing secondary actors to weaponize the tooling against additional targets.

timeline.event: Malicious pull request submitted to TanStack repository fork, triggering CI/CD cache poisoning.
timeline.significance: Initiates automated pipeline hijacking and OIDC token scraping.

timeline.event: 84 malicious TanStack npm packages published; dead man's switch mechanism confirmed.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates large-scale distribution and destructive persistence capabilities.

timeline.event: TeamPCP open-sources Mini Shai-Hulud worm on GitHub; Checkmarx Jenkins plugin sabotaged.
timeline.significance: Ensures rapid proliferation and expands attack surface to security tooling.

timeline.event: Mistral AI repositories advertised for sale; additional npm/PyPI packages compromised.
timeline.significance: Confirms financial motivation and ongoing campaign against AI/ML infrastructure.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Global developer ecosystem, AI/ML pipelines, and cloud infrastructure compromised. Massive credential theft, data destruction risk, and supply chain contamination.
threat_stories.action_required: Audit and harden GitHub Actions workflow permissions; disable pull_request_target for untrusted forks. Pin dependency versions in lockfiles and scan for known compromised packages. Rotate all cloud credentials, GitHub tokens, and API keys active during the infection window. Isolate CI/CD runners in ephemeral environments and implement strict OIDC token scope restrictions.

threat_stories.headline: The Control Plane Firestorm: Active Exploitation of Critical Network Infrastructure
threat_stories.narrative:
Critical network and identity infrastructure is under immediate, coordinated assault. Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS firewalls have been exploited for weeks via a zero-day in the User-ID Authentication Portal, granting unauthenticated root access to thousands of internet-facing devices. Simultaneously, Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN controllers face active exploitation of an authentication bypass flaw that grants full administrative access to vSmart and vManage services. Compounding the crisis, an 18-year-old heap buffer overflow in NGINX’s rewrite module and a critical authentication bypass in Quest KACE SMA are being weaponized.

These are not theoretical risks; threat actors are deploying automated scanners and exploiting these flaws in real-time to establish persistent network control. The PAN-OS vulnerability allows direct remote code execution with root privileges, while the Cisco flaw enables attackers to inject SSH keys and manipulate SD-WAN network configurations across the fabric. The NGINX vulnerability, hidden for nearly two decades, triggers deterministic heap overflows when specific rewrite directives are combined, enabling remote code execution or denial of service.

The convergence of these flaws creates a perfect storm for enterprise network compromise. Attackers can pivot from perimeter firewalls to SD-WAN controllers, then to web infrastructure and endpoint management systems, achieving complete lateral movement without triggering traditional alerts. Immediate isolation and patching are required to prevent widespread operational disruption.

timeline.event: PAN-OS User-ID Authentication Portal zero-day exploitation confirmed; 5,400+ firewalls exposed.
timeline.significance: Establishes active exploitation of perimeter defense infrastructure with root-level access.

timeline.event: Quest KACE SMA authentication bypass patched; active exploitation noted in wild.
timeline.significance: Highlights vulnerability of endpoint management appliances to unauthenticated takeover.

timeline.event: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN auth bypass added to CISA KEV catalog; active attacks confirmed.
timeline.significance: Elevates SD-WAN controller compromise to critical infrastructure risk.

timeline.event: NGINX 18-year-old RCE (CVE-2026-42945) disclosed; proof-of-concept published.
timeline.significance: Exposes widespread web infrastructure to deterministic remote code execution.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Enterprise networks, cloud gateways, SD-WAN fabrics, and web infrastructure face complete compromise. Unauthenticated remote code execution and administrative takeover are actively occurring.
threat_stories.action_required: Restrict PAN-OS User-ID portal access to trusted management IPs only; disable captive portal if unused. Patch Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN controllers immediately and rotate all SSH keys. Upgrade NGINX to version 1.31.0 or 1.30.1 and replace unnamed PCRE captures with named captures. Isolate Quest KACE appliances and enforce strict network segmentation. Deploy virtual patching where native updates are delayed.

actor_profiles.name: ShinyHunters
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Executed massive breach of Instructure Canvas LMS, exfiltrating 3.65TB of data across 9,000 institutions. Defaced login portals, disrupted final exams, and forced ransom payment after demonstrating persistent re-entry capabilities.

actor_profiles.name: TeamPCP
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Conducted automated CI/CD pipeline poisoning campaign using cache-poisoning techniques. Compromised TanStack, Mistral AI, Guardrails AI, and Checkmarx repositories. Distributed trojanized npm/PyPI packages featuring credential stealers and destructive dead man's switches.

actor_profiles.name: FamousSparrow
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Conducted multi-wave intrusion campaign against Azerbaijani energy sector. Leveraged ProxyNotShell exploit chain to compromise Microsoft Exchange Server, deploying web shells and custom malware loaders for persistent access and data exfiltration.

actor_profiles.name: Nitrogen Ransomware
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Claimed massive cyberattack against Foxconn's North American factories. Stole confidential files related to Apple and Nvidia supply chains, deployed ransomware payloads, and disrupted manufacturing operations.

actor_profiles.name: Kimsuky
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deployed PebbleDash and AppleSeed malware clusters via spear-phishing. Abused Visual Studio Code Remote Tunneling for covert C2 access and deployed DWAgent RMM tool for persistent remote administration. Exfiltrated GPKI certificates, screenshots, and keystrokes.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Fragnesia is a local privilege escalation vulnerability in the XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. It allows unprivileged users to corrupt page cache memory, modifying protected file data in RAM without altering disk contents, enabling deterministic root access.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply vendor kernel patches immediately. Disable esp4 and esp6 modules if ESP-in-TCP is not required. Reboot systems to load patched kernels.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Dirty Frag exploit chain chaining IPsec ESP and RxRPC protocol flaws. Enables local privilege escalation by abusing memory fragmentation handling to corrupt page caches and overwrite protected files.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply official kernel patches released May 8-11, 2026. Blocklist esp4, esp6, and rxrpc kernel modules as temporary mitigation.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical authentication bypass flaw allowing remote attackers to skip login verification, gain administrative access, deploy Filemanager backdoors, install SSH keys, and exfiltrate data via Telegram.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update cPanel/WHM to latest patched versions immediately. Rotate all credentials, audit SSH keys, and hunt for webshells/backdoor artifacts.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: 18-year-old heap buffer overflow in ngx_http_rewrite_module. Triggered by specific rewrite directives with unnamed PCRE captures and question marks, enabling remote code execution or denial of service.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to NGINX 1.31.0 or 1.30.1. Replace unnamed PCRE captures with named captures. Restart NGINX to reload patched workers.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authentication bypass in peering authentication mechanism allows unauthenticated remote attackers to gain administrative privileges, inject SSH keys, and manipulate SD-WAN network configurations.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to latest fixed software version immediately. Restrict network access to vSmart/vManage services. Rotate all authorized keys.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical authentication bypass in SSO handling mechanism allowing unauthenticated attackers to impersonate administrators, extract database dumps, and compromise managed endpoints.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply security patch released May 2025. Restrict internet-facing exposure and isolate management consoles via network segmentation.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Missing authentication in legacy Flask API server. Hardcoded AUTH_ENABLED=False causes protected routes to fail open, allowing unauthenticated access to agent workflows and sensitive endpoints.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to PraisonAI 4.6.34+. Discontinue use of legacy api_server.py. Restrict network access and enforce authentication on all endpoints.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: SQL injection in proxy API key verification process. Unauthenticated attackers can send crafted Authorization headers to read/modify database contents, including virtual API keys and provider credentials.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade LiteLLM to version 1.83.7+. Enable disable_error_logs:true to block attack path. Rotate all compromised API keys.

hunting_leads.title: Hunt for Dirty Frag/Fragnesia LPE Activity
hunting_leads.context: These exploits modify page cache memory without touching disk, bypassing traditional file integrity monitoring. Hunt for anomalous kernel module loading, seccomp violations, and privilege escalation attempts.
hunting_leads.query: | tstats summariesonly=t allow_old_summaries=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name IN ("modprobe", "insmod", "rmmod") AND Processes.process="*esp4*" OR Processes.process="*esp6*" OR Processes.process="*rxrpc*" by Processes.process_name Processes.dest Processes.user span=1h
indicators: Loading of esp4/esp6/rxrpc modules
Unexpected su/sudo execution from unprivileged users
Seccomp profile violations
Modification of /usr/bin/su or /etc/passwd in memory

hunting_leads.title: Hunt for TeamPCP Mini Shai-Hulud Supply Chain Compromise
hunting_leads.context: The malware scrapes OIDC tokens from CI/CD runners, installs dead man's switches as system services, and monitors GitHub tokens. Hunt for anomalous service creation, token scraping, and disk wipe commands.
hunting_leads.query: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name IN ("systemd", "svchost", "npm", "pip") AND (Processes.process="*dead*" OR Processes.process="*wipe*" OR Processes.process="*oidc*" OR Processes.process="*token*") by Processes.dest Processes.user span=1h
indicators: Creation of system services named 'deadman' or similar
npm/pip installs of tanstack/mistral/guardrails packages on May 11-12
Outbound connections to jsonkeeper.com or attacker C2
Execution of rm -rf ~ or equivalent disk wipe commands

hunting_leads.title: Hunt for ShinyHunters Canvas Lateral Movement
hunting_leads.context: Attackers abused Canvas export features, API tokens, and Free-for-Teacher accounts. Hunt for bulk data exports, anomalous token generation, and portal file modifications.
hunting_leads.query: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Web.Proxy where Web.url LIKE "%canvas*export%" OR Web.url LIKE "%canvas*api/token%" OR Web.url LIKE "%canvas*login%" by Web.src Web.dest Web.url span=15m
indicators: Massive CSV/JSON exports from Canvas APIs
Creation of new admin-level API tokens
Modification of login portal HTML/CSS files
Authentication attempts from known ShinyHunters IP ranges

hunting_leads.title: Hunt for AI-Generated 2FA Bypass Attempts
hunting_leads.context: AI-generated exploits target semantic logic flaws in authentication flows. Hunt for Python script execution during auth, anomalous bypass patterns, and hallucinated CVSS artifacts in logs.
hunting_leads.query: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication where Authentication.auth_result="Success" AND Authentication.process_name="python*" OR Authentication.process_name="*.py" by Authentication.dest Authentication.user Authentication.process_name span=1h
indicators: Python scripts executing during authentication workflows
Successful 2FA bypass without valid second factor
Log entries containing 'CVSS' or educational strings in exploit payloads
Anomalous session token generation patterns

hunting_leads.title: Hunt for NGINX Rewrite Module Exploitation
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-42945 is triggered by specific URI patterns combining rewrite directives, unnamed PCRE captures, and question marks. Hunt for HTTP 502/504 spikes and malformed request patterns.
hunting_leads.query: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Web.Proxy where Web.status IN (502, 504, 500) AND Web.url LIKE "%$1%" OR Web.url LIKE "%$2%" OR Web.url LIKE "%?%" by Web.src Web.dest Web.url span=5m
indicators: Sudden spikes in HTTP 502/504 errors on NGINX endpoints
URIs containing $1, $2, or ? in query strings targeting rewrite rules
Worker process crashes or restarts
Outbound connections to attacker infrastructure post-exploitation

top_vulnerabilities: {'summary': 'Microsoft Edge loads the entire password vault into plaintext process memory at startup, remaining accessible for the session duration.', 'severity': 'medium', 'affected_products': ['Microsoft Edge'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': 0.0, 'workarounds': ['Disable password saving in Microsoft Edge', 'Turn off autofill functionality', 'Use a dedicated third-party password manager', 'Enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on all accounts', 'Restrict local administrator privileges to limit process memory access'], 'references': ['Malwarebytes: Microsoft says Edge’s plaintext password behavior is “by design” (2026-05-08)', '2024 Academic Study on Password Manager Memory Leaks'], 'integrity_impact': 'none', 'description': "Unlike other Chromium-based browsers that decrypt passwords on-demand and utilize app-bound encryption, Microsoft Edge loads the complete password vault into plaintext within its process memory upon startup. This design choice, acknowledged by Microsoft as 'by design' to optimize sign-in and autofill performance, significantly lowers the barrier for post-compromise credential harvesting. Attackers with local code execution and elevated privileges can read the process memory to extract all stored credentials without requiring zero-day exploits or complex techniques. This behavior aligns with capabilities already present in many infostealer malware families.", 'attack_vector': 'local', 'privileges_required': 'high', 'exploit_maturity': 'proof-of-concept', 'availability_impact': 'none', 'remediation': "Microsoft has not issued a patch as this is considered a design decision. statistics.key_changes: AI-driven vulnerability discovery has compressed the patch lifecycle from months to hours. Linux kernel stability is under unprecedented stress with three related LPEs in three weeks. Supply chain attacks have shifted from direct repository compromise to automated CI/CD pipeline poisoning. Educational and network control plane infrastructure face coordinated, high-impact breaches.
emerging_trends: AI-generated zero-day exploits targeting semantic logic flaws and 2FA bypass
Autonomous AI agent privilege escalation and prompt injection attacks
Dead man's switches in supply chain malware to destroy forensic evidence
In-memory privilege escalation bypassing traditional file integrity monitoring
Automated exploitation of network control planes (SD-WAN, firewalls, web servers)
declining_threats: Traditional signature-based malware detection (bypassed by fileless/memory-only techniques)
Manual vulnerability scanning and periodic patching cycles (outpaced by AI discovery)
Static posture-based AI security controls (brittle against dynamic agent behavior)
Isolated supply chain attacks (replaced by automated, pipeline-wide poisoning)
